content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Why what you put in the context window matters more than how much you can fit.

Context windows keep getting bigger. 128K tokens. 200K. A million. The race toward larger context is treated as a race toward solving memory — as if the problem with LLM workflows was always one of capacity.
It isn’t. And bigger windows are making that more obvious, not less.

The capacity trap
When context windows were small, the constraint was forced on you. You couldn’t fit everything, so you had to choose. Choosing was painful, but it was also clarifying — every token had to earn its place.
Larger windows removed that constraint. And removing it didn’t remove the underlying problem. It hid it.
Teams now routinely load tens of thousands of tokens into a single session — full documentation, complete conversation histories, every file that might be relevant. The model technically can hold all of it. What nobody asks is whether it should.
This is the capacity trap: mistaking what fits for what matters. A context window that can hold 100,000 tokens is not the same as a context window that should.

What actually happens when you flood the context
Loading more content into context does not produce proportionally better outputs. Past a certain point, it produces worse ones.
This isn’t a model failing to “read” its context — it’s reading all of it, faithfully. The problem is allocation. A model with 50,000 tokens of loosely relevant material and 200 tokens of explicit priority signals has no reliable way to know which 200 tokens should dominate its behavior. Everything competes for attention on roughly equal footing, and equal footing is exactly the wrong distribution for a workflow that has actual priorities.
Related phenomena have been described under names such as context degradation or context rot: the information is technically present, but its influence on the output degrades as the surrounding volume grows. The content isn’t lost. It’s drowned.
This matters because it inverts the intuitive fix. The instinct, when an LLM workflow misbehaves, is to add more context — more background, more history, more detail, on the theory that the model just needs more information to get it right. Frequently, this makes things worse. The model now has to extract the same signal from a noisier field.

Attention is not memory
This is where a category error creeps into how most people think about context windows.
Memory is about what can be stored and retrieved. Attention is about what gets weighted, prioritized, and acted on. A workflow can have excellent memory — everything saved, nothing lost — and still fail completely at attention, because nothing in that saved material signals what should take precedence right now.
Treating the context window purely as memory leads to a specific failure pattern: teams build elaborate retrieval systems to make sure nothing relevant is ever missing, and then wonder why the model still produces inconsistent, drifting outputs. The retrieval worked. Everything relevant was there. But “everything relevant” is not the same as “what should govern this response,” and no amount of retrieval sophistication closes that gap on its own.
This is not a critique of context engineering — the practice of deciding what content enters the window, in what form, in what order. Context engineering is necessary and genuinely difficult work. But it answers a different question than the one that determines whether a workflow stays coherent over time. Context engineering asks what the model should see. It does not, by itself, answer what the model should treat as authoritative once it sees it.

The scarcity that doesn’t go away
Here is the part that larger context windows don’t fix: attention remains scarce even when capacity is abundant.
A model can process 200,000 tokens. It cannot treat 200,000 tokens as equally important. Something will dominate the response, whether or not anyone designed for that outcome. If nothing was deliberately weighted, the model falls back on its own defaults — recency, repetition, surface salience — none of which reliably correspond to what the operator actually needs prioritized.
This is the sense in which attention is the scarce resource, independent of context length. Capacity is a property of the model. Attention allocation is a property of how the content is structured. Scaling the first does nothing to solve the second.
A useful comparison: adding RAM to a computer does not organize the files on disk. It just means you can load more of them into memory at once — disorganized files included. The organizing has to happen somewhere else, deliberately, or scale just means more disorder loaded faster.

What bounded attention looks like
A workflow that treats attention as a governed resource looks different from one that treats the context window as a container to fill.
The difference comes down to a small number of design choices, applied consistently:
A small set of items stays consistently high-priority. Not everything that’s relevant — only what must reliably govern behavior. If everything is flagged as important, nothing is. A workflow with twenty “critical” constraints has, in practice, none.
Priority is explicit, not inferred. The model should not have to guess what matters most from position in the conversation or frequency of mention. A structure that states “this is active, this is current, this is non-negotiable” removes the guessing. A pile of relevant facts, however accurate, leaves the weighting to chance.
Old information is allowed to recede. Not deleted — recede. A decision made three months ago and never revisited does not need the same attention weight as one confirmed this week. A workflow that treats all retained information as equally live is a workflow that never lets anything age appropriately, which means everything competes at full volume indefinitely.
Growth in content does not mean growth in priority. As a project accumulates history, the volume of potentially relevant material grows continuously. The volume of what should actively govern behavior does not have to grow at the same rate — and design that assumes otherwise produces a context window that gets noisier, not more useful, the longer a project runs.
None of this eliminates the underlying scarcity. It manages it. A workflow built this way still can’t make everything equally important — nothing can. What it does is make the prioritization itself a deliberate, inspectable choice instead of an emergent accident of however much content happened to get loaded.

What this does not promise
Bounding attention this way is a design property, not a performance guarantee. It does not make a model immune to losing track of priorities in a long enough session, and it does not produce a fixed, predictable cost regardless of how a workflow is used. What changes is the default: a structure with a small number of explicitly prioritized items degrades more gracefully and more legibly than one where priority was never made explicit in the first place. That is a meaningful difference in practice. It is not a ceiling on what can go wrong.

Why this connects to governance, not just engineering
This is where attention management and governance turn out to be the same problem, viewed from different angles.
Governance asks what a workflow should treat as authoritative. Attention is the mechanism through which that authority actually reaches the model — the channel by which “this matters” becomes something the model can act on rather than something written down and ignored. A governance structure that never gets prioritized in context is not actually governing anything. It is just text the model happens to also be holding.
This is why bounding attention cannot be solved purely as a retrieval problem or purely as a context-engineering problem. Retrieval determines what’s available. Context engineering determines how it’s packaged. Neither one, on its own, determines what should dominate the model’s behavior once everything is in front of it. That is a governance question, answered by structure, not by volume.
A context window with a thousand tokens of well-prioritized governance structure can be operationally more effective than one with fifty thousand tokens of accurate but unweighted information. Not because the larger window contains less truth — it may contain more — but because truth without priority does not reliably translate into the behavior an operator actually needs.

The resource that scaling doesn’t solve
Context windows will keep growing. That is a genuine, useful trend — more room means more flexibility, fewer hard trade-offs, less aggressive pruning forced by sheer lack of space.
What it will not do is make prioritization unnecessary. A bigger container does not organize what’s inside it. As long as a model has to act on a finite set of priorities at any given moment — and it always will, regardless of how many tokens fit — attention stays scarce even when capacity stops being the bottleneck.
The teams that get this right are not the ones with the most sophisticated retrieval, or the largest context windows. They are the ones who decided, deliberately and explicitly, what should never get lost in the noise — and built a structure that keeps it that way as everything else grows around it.
Capacity scales. Attention doesn’t.

This article is part of a series on FIL — a governance protocol for persistent LLM workflows. FIL explores one possible approach to governing attention in persistent LLM workflows. It is not a context engineering framework or a performance optimization technique. It is an attempt to make priority explicit as workflows grow over time.

Note: FIL is an independent, open-source personal research project. The methodologies, frameworks, and opinions expressed in this article are solely my own and do not represent, engage, or reflect the views of my employer.
